sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated a thin-lens camera model used to focus objects placed at different distances while lens focal length was fixed to connect a measurable physical trend with a the physical sciences principle. The central idea was that object distance, image distance, and focal length are connected by the thin-lens equation. Before collecting data, the team identified the governing relationship as 1/f = 1/do + 1/di and magnification m = -di/do. This mattered because the same topic vocabulary could support several tempting but incompatible answers unless the variables, units, and controlled conditions were kept straight. The investigators therefore designed the experiment so that the figure had to be interpreted through proportionality, limiting behavior, and the direction of causality rather than through sentence recall alone.

The experimental manipulation involved moving the object closer to the lens while measuring the image distance needed to restore a sharp image. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: image distance increased as object distance approached the focal length from beyond it. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that focal length, lens alignment, aperture, and screen position calibration were controlled. Those controls were important because a change in a calibration setting, environmental condition, sample geometry, or hidden concentration could create an apparent trend without testing the intended physical relationship. A correct explanation must therefore account for both what was deliberately changed and what was explicitly held constant.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that moving an object closer always moves the real image closer to the lens for a converging lens. Another was that a real image forms at the focal point for every object distance. Each alternative contains a familiar phrase from chemistry or physics, so it could attract a student who recognizes a term but does not check whether the prediction matches the graph. The investigators rejected these explanations because they either reverse the expected direction, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore a conserved quantity, or attribute the effect to a controlled variable.

The follow-up analysis asked students to make a prediction from the same relationship. For this passage, the key is to connect the measured trend to thin-lens camera focus, translate the graph into the appropriate mathematical dependency, and use the controls to rule out artifacts. The relevant calculation is intentionally modest; the harder part is deciding which variable belongs in the numerator, denominator, exponent, logarithm, or proportionality.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the thin-lens camera focus experiment?

choices.A: Moving an object closer always moves the real image closer to the lens for a converging lens
choices.B: A real image forms at the focal point for every object distance, even though the passage states that focal length was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure
choices.C: The result is best explained by the thin-lens relationship requiring a larger image distance as object distance decreases toward the focal length
choices.D: The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls

explanations.A: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that Moving an object closer always moves the real image closer to the lens for a converging lens conflicts with the stated relationship, 1/f = 1/do + 1/di and magnification m = -di/do, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that image distance increased as object distance approached the focal length from beyond it and states that focal length, lens alignment, aperture, and screen position calibration were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.B: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that A real image forms at the focal point for every object distance, even though the passage states that focal length was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure conflicts with the stated relationship, 1/f = 1/do + 1/di and magnification m = -di/do, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that image distance increased as object distance approached the focal length from beyond it and states that focal length, lens alignment, aperture, and screen position calibration were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.C: Correct. This answer accounts for the manipulation, the plotted trend, and the controlled variables at the same time. The passage identifies the relevant relationship as 1/f = 1/do + 1/di and magnification m = -di/do, so the reasoning depends on how the variables are related rather than on a keyword match. In this experiment, image distance increased as object distance approached the focal length from beyond it, while focal length, lens alignment, aperture, and screen position calibration were controlled. That pattern supports this choice because The result is best explained by the thin-lens relationship requiring a larger image distance as object distance decreases toward the focal length. It also predicts the correct follow-up behavior if the same variable is changed again. The tempting alternatives either reverse the dependency, mistake a controlled variable for the causal variable, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuse a measured signal with the physical quantity that produced it.
explanations.D: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls conflicts with the stated relationship, 1/f = 1/do + 1/di and magnification m = -di/do, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that image distance increased as object distance approached the focal length from beyond it and states that focal length, lens alignment, aperture, and screen position calibration were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.B: Geometric optics predicts image formation from object distance, image distance, and focal length
choices.C: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.D: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent, even though the passage states that focal length was controlled and the measured trend moved systematically in the figure

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated a slit-based optical setup designed to determine how aperture width affects the angular spread of a diffraction pattern to connect a measurable physical trend with a the physical sciences principle. The central idea was that diffraction spreading increases with wavelength and decreases with aperture width. Before collecting data, the team identified the governing relationship as for the first minimum, sinθ ≈ λ/a. This mattered because the same topic vocabulary could support several tempting but incompatible answers unless the variables, units, and controlled conditions were kept straight. The investigators therefore designed the experiment so that the figure had to be interpreted through proportionality, limiting behavior, and the direction of causality rather than through sentence recall alone.

The experimental manipulation involved decreasing slit width while using the same monochromatic light source. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: narrower slits produced wider central maxima. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that wavelength, screen distance, slit alignment, and light intensity calibration were controlled. Those controls were important because a change in a calibration setting, environmental condition, sample geometry, or hidden concentration could create an apparent trend without testing the intended physical relationship. A correct explanation must therefore account for both what was deliberately changed and what was explicitly held constant.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that wider apertures cause more diffraction because more rays can bend through the opening. Another was that diffraction disappears for visible light because light travels in straight lines only. Each alternative contains a familiar phrase from chemistry or physics, so it could attract a student who recognizes a term but does not check whether the prediction matches the graph. The investigators rejected these explanations because they either reverse the expected direction, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore a conserved quantity, or attribute the effect to a controlled variable.

The follow-up analysis asked students to make a prediction from the same relationship. For this passage, the key is to connect the measured trend to diffraction resolution assay, translate the graph into the appropriate mathematical dependency, and use the controls to rule out artifacts. The relevant calculation is intentionally modest; the harder part is deciding which variable belongs in the numerator, denominator, exponent, logarithm, or proportionality.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the diffraction resolution assay experiment?

choices.A: The pattern is best explained by the inverse relationship between slit width and angular spread
choices.B: Wider apertures cause more diffraction because more rays can bend through the opening
choices.C: Diffraction disappears for visible light because light travels in straight lines only
choices.D: The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls

explanations.A: Correct. This answer accounts for the manipulation, the plotted trend, and the controlled variables at the same time. The passage identifies the relevant relationship as for the first minimum, sinθ ≈ λ/a, so the reasoning depends on how the variables are related rather than on a keyword match. In this experiment, narrower slits produced wider central maxima, while wavelength, screen distance, slit alignment, and light intensity calibration were controlled. That pattern supports this choice because The pattern is best explained by the inverse relationship between slit width and angular spread. It also predicts the correct follow-up behavior if the same variable is changed again. The tempting alternatives either reverse the dependency, mistake a controlled variable for the causal variable, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuse a measured signal with the physical quantity that produced it.
explanations.B: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that Wider apertures cause more diffraction because more rays can bend through the opening conflicts with the stated relationship, for the first minimum, sinθ ≈ λ/a, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that narrower slits produced wider central maxima and states that wavelength, screen distance, slit alignment, and light intensity calibration were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.C: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design.
